sections.title: What it is
sections.content: On brick, mold often shows in mortar joints and face pores where water wicked in and dried unevenly. Living or once-living biological film that can etch finishes if left untreated. Because brick is porous, the issue may look deeper than a simple surface film. On brick, mold often shows in mortar joints and face pores where water wicked in and dried unevenly. sections.title: Why it happens
sections.content: Brick pulls moisture through capillaries; salts and organics migrate to the drying face and deposit mold. Humidity, organic food film, and poor ventilation extend damp dwell time microbes need. Freeze–thaw and irrigation splash accelerate cycling damage. sections.content: On glazed shower tile, mold usually bonds to the glaze film, grout perimeter, or texture low points. Living or once-living biological film that can etch finishes if left untreated. sections.content: Residual soap, minerals, and body soils thin-spread across tile but drain into grout joints where mold concentrates.   Lower spots stay wet longest. Behind faucets, along lower courses, and near cook lines—mold on shower tile tracks where spray, steam, and spills concentrate daily.

sections.title: How to fix it
sections.content: Clean tile faces with non-scratch pads and a product safe for the glaze; focus mold removal at edges and caulk transitions.
